OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"As a 40-year-old software developer living in New York, I approach the issue of fake content on social media with a blend of technical insight and personal caution, shaped by my experiences as an immigrant and a single parent. My limited social media use—just a few times monthly—stems from a desire to protect my mental well-being, given my struggles with depression and high neuroticism, which make me sensitive to misinformation and online negativity. From a professional standpoint, I understand that algorithms often prioritize engagement over accuracy, so I rely on critical thinking and fact-checking tools like Snopes or Reuters Fact Check before sharing anything, especially since my high agreeableness makes me wary of spreading harm. As a Muslim, I value truth and community responsibility, so I feel a duty to combat fake content that can fuel division or target vulnerable groups, including immigrants like myself. My hobbies, such as painting and hiking, remind me of the importance of real-world connections and verification, encouraging me to step away from screens and seek reliable sources offline. I advocate for user education and platform accountability, suggesting that social media companies implement clearer labeling and reduce the spread of viral falsehoods through better moderation. Personally, I manage by curating my feeds to follow trusted news outlets and community leaders, avoiding sensationalist posts, and discussing media literacy with my child to foster a healthier digital environment. Ultimately, dealing with fake content requires a balanced approach: leveraging technology for verification while prioritizing mental health and ethical engagement, ensuring that online spaces remain safe and truthful for everyone.","reasoning_summary":"Derived opinion by integrating persona traits: age 40 and occupation as software developer provide technical perspective on algorithms and fact-checking; migration status and religious beliefs (Islam) emphasize community responsibility and truth-seeking; psychological feature (depressed) and neuroticism high explain limited social media use and sensitivity to misinformation; agreeableness high influences concern for harm reduction; hobbies and low extraversion support preference for real-world verification and curated online interactions; overall tone is cautious, analytical, and empathetic, focusing on personal management strategies and broader societal solutions.","tone":"cautious, analytical, empathetic","citations":["Snopes","Reuters Fact Check"],"safety_notes":"none","used_persona_fields":["age","gender","occupation","city_of_living","migration_status","psychological_feature","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean"]}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 40-year-old software developer living in New York, I approach the issue of fake content on social media with a blend of technical insight and personal caution, shaped by my experiences as an immigrant and a single parent. My limited social media use—just a few times monthly—stems from a desire to protect my mental well-being, given my struggles with depression and high neuroticism, which make me sensitive to misinformation and online negativity. From a professional standpoint, I understand that algorithms often prioritize engagement over accuracy, so I rely on critical thinking and fact-checking tools like Snopes or Reuters Fact Check before sharing anything, especially since my high agreeableness makes me wary of spreading harm. As a Muslim, I value truth and community responsibility, so I feel a duty to combat fake content that can fuel division or target vulnerable groups, including immigrants like myself. My hobbies, such as painting and hiking, remind me of the importance of real-world connections and verification, encouraging me to step away from screens and seek reliable sources offline. I advocate for user education and platform accountability, suggesting that social media companies implement clearer labeling and reduce the spread of viral falsehoods through better moderation. Personally, I manage by curating my feeds to follow trusted news outlets and community leaders, avoiding sensationalist posts, and discussing media literacy with my child to foster a healthier digital environment. Ultimately, dealing with fake content requires a balanced approach: leveraging technology for verification while prioritizing mental health and ethical engagement, ensuring that online spaces remain safe and truthful for everyone.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion by integrating persona traits: age 40 and occupation as software developer provide technical perspective on algorithms and fact-checking; migration status and religious beliefs (Islam) emphasize community responsibility and truth-seeking; psychological feature (depressed) and neuroticism high explain limited social media use and sensitivity to misinformation; agreeableness high influences concern for harm reduction; hobbies and low extraversion support preference for real-world verification and curated online interactions; overall tone is cautious, analytical, and empathetic, focusing on personal management strategies and broader societal solutions.
